title: Understanding ILITs
desc: How Irrevocable Life Insurance Trusts shield your estate from taxes.
content: An Irrevocable Life Insurance Trust (ILIT) is a specialized trust designed to own and manage life insurance policies. By moving the policy out of your personal estate, the death benefit is generally protected from federal and state estate taxes.

sections.heading: Tax Shield Mechanics
sections.text: When an ILIT owns the policy, the proceeds are removed from your taxable estate. This is critical for high-net-worth individuals to avoid the 40% federal estate tax on life insurance proceeds.

sections.heading: The 3-Year Rule
sections.text: If you transfer an existing policy into an ILIT, you must survive for three years after the transfer for the proceeds to be excluded from your estate. Direct purchases by the trust avoid this rule.

sections.heading: Crummey Powers
sections.text: To qualify for the annual gift tax exclusion, beneficiaries must be given 'Crummey' withdrawal rights for a specific period (usually 30 days) after each contribution to the trust.

title: Infinite Banking 101
desc: The mechanics of borrowing against your own policy cash value.
content: IBC involves using a high-cash-value policy as a personal bank. You borrow against the policy's cash value while your full balance continues to earn dividends and interest.

sections.heading: Non-Direct Recognition
sections.text: In a non-direct recognition policy, the insurance company pays the same dividend rate on the entire cash value, including the portion used as collateral for a loan.

sections.heading: Uninterrupted Compounding
sections.text: Because the cash value itself never leaves the policy, it continues to compound based on the full value, while the loan interest is paid separately to the carrier.

sections.heading: Debt-to-Equity Control
sections.text: Maintaining a healthy loan-to-value (LTV) ratio (under 90%) ensures the policy remains in-force and continues to grow your wealth base.

title: Legal Forms by State
desc: Essential documentation for recording and maintaining your trust.
content: Each state has specific requirements for trust recording and asset assignment. Understanding these is vital for judicial compliance.

sections.heading: North Carolina / Georgia
sections.text: Detailed Table of Contents and Portfolio Indexing is required for physical recording in the Superior Court system. Certificate of Trust is often preferred over filing the full document.

sections.heading: Florida / Texas
sections.text: Notice of Trust must be filed in the county of the settlor's domicile. Affidavit of Trust is commonly used to transfer title of assets to the trustee.

sections.heading: California / New York
sections.text: Trust Transfer Deeds and PCOR (Preliminary Change of Ownership Report) are required when funding real estate into the trust. Stringent Crummey notification proof is recommended.

resources.name: NC Judicial Branch - Estate & Trust Forms

title: Processing a Trust Loan
desc: Step-by-step SOP for accessing liquidity from the trust.
content: Learn the standard operating procedure for requesting a collateralized loan from your insurance-funded trust.

sections.heading: Step 1: Board Resolution
sections.text: The Trustee must document a formal resolution authorizing the loan from the trust's life insurance policy for trust-related investments or expenses.

sections.heading: Step 2: Carrier Request
sections.text: Submit the standard loan request form to the insurance carrier. Request 'Fixed Rate' if available to lock in borrowing costs.

sections.heading: Step 3: Fund Distribution
sections.text: Once the carrier issues funds (directly into the Trust Bank Account), the Trustee should record the inflow as a 'Policy Loan Liability' for accounting purposes.

resources.name: Standard Life Insurance Policy Loan Request Example

title: Top Funding Carriers
desc: Prominent mutual insurance companies specializing in IBC-style policies.
content: Not all life insurance companies are suited for Trust Funding or Infinite Banking. Prominent institutions are typically mutually owned (owned by policyholders, not shareholders) and explicitly allow non-direct recognition policy loans.

sections.heading: MassMutual (Massachusetts Mutual)
sections.text: A heavily utilized, highly-rated mutual company known for strong historical dividend payouts and flexible Paid-Up Additions (PUA) riders essential for rapid cash value growth.

sections.heading: Penn Mutual
sections.text: Favored by many IBC practitioners for their direct recognition and non-direct recognition options, allowing policyholders to choose how their loans impact dividend crediting based on market environments.

sections.heading: Lafayette Life
sections.text: A smaller but highly specialized mutual company out of the Western & Southern Financial Group, known for strict adherence to non-direct recognition and excellent IBC-friendly whole life chassis.

sections.heading: Guardian Life
sections.text: Known for an exceptionally strong Whole Life portfolio. While Guardian uses direct recognition, their 'Index Participation Feature' is popular for sophisticated trust strategies.

title: IBC Policy Types
desc: Understanding the different insurance policies pitched for Infinite Banking.
content: While several types of life insurance are pitched for Infinite Banking (IBC), only one aligns with the core principles of safety, predictability, and guarantees: Dividend-Paying Whole Life from a Mutual Company.

sections.heading: Dividend-Paying Whole Life (The Gold Standard)
sections.text: The only type strictly recommended by IBC purists. It offers guaranteed level premiums, a guaranteed death benefit, and guaranteed cash value accumulation. Dividends purchase Paid-Up Additions (PUAs) to rapidly accelerate growth without market risk.

sections.heading: Indexed Universal Life (IUL)
sections.text: Often pitched for higher returns, but lacks core guarantees. Cash value growth is tied to a market index subject to caps and participation rates that the carrier can change. It features rising internal costs that can erode cash value during flat market years.

sections.heading: Universal Life (UL)
sections.text: Builds cash value based on a declared interest rate set by the carrier. Similar to IULs, the cost of insurance increases over time. It lacks the long-term guarantees required for a stable banking system and can implode if costs consume the cash value.

sections.heading: Variable Universal Life (VUL)
sections.text: Cash value is invested directly into market sub-accounts, violating the IBC principle of safety. Market downturns can cause cash value to drop. If you have a loan during a crash, it can trigger a margin call or cause policy lapse with massive tax consequences.
